text: The surgeon introduces the optical portal using the rule of the palm, positioning the top edge of the palm under the trapezoid space to facilitate visualization during the mobilization phase.

The surgeon uses an atraumatic trocar to penetrate the abdominal cavity, visualizing the area with a laparoscopic camera. Insufflation is initiated, maintaining a pressure of fourteen to fifteen millimeters of mercury. The trocar is secured with a surgical drape.

The surgeon places a twelve-millimeter port slightly lateral to the line of the sternocleidomastoid muscle for the use of staplers. Another twelve-millimeter working port is positioned near the duodenum. Bipolar forceps are prepared for use, and adjustments to the feed are made.

The surgeon uses gravity as a passive assistant while inserting a bipolar instrument through a five-millimeter port. A second retractor is positioned on the Tanson, allowing for deeper access from the edge of the surgical field.

The surgical team prepares the standard port placement for a gastrectomy, including a sub-xiphoidal port and an optical port at the first stage of the operation. The patient is positioned slightly tilted to the left in the Fowler position, with visible anatomical landmarks such as the left rib and middle line marked for reference.

The surgeon approaches the sixth group of lymph nodes, carefully avoiding opening the mesocolon, which appears slightly adhered. The assistant uses a grasper to perform traction on the stomach, while the surgeon provides counter-traction to facilitate the dissection.
The surgeon navigates into the fusion fascia layer, approaching the sixth group of lymph nodes. The mesocolon is visible, and a vessel is identified nearby.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel near the pancreas, adjusting the position slightly to the right side for better access and visibility.
The camera zooms in to provide a closer view of the pancreas, highlighting its position and texture within the surgical field.

The surgeon zooms in on the pancreas and clavicle, preparing to ligate vessels using a combination of electrosurgery and clips. The assistant provides traction and adjusts the position under the surgeon's guidance, focusing on the abdominal wall rather than cranially.

The surgeon attempts to manage torn clips below the surgical field, focusing on the sixth group of lymph nodes. The lymph node is visually identified and manipulated, with attention given to its proximity to surrounding structures.

The surgeon positions scissors to protect the stomach, preparing to mobilize surrounding tissue. The scissors are placed under a device, and the surgeon navigates to the left, identifying lymph node groups five, seven, and eight.

The surgeon uses a right clamp positioned in the patient's left rib area and a left clamp oriented hour-long, maintaining triangulation throughout the operation. The surgical field includes the mesogastrium and lateral pubic region, with a background gallbladder disease noted. The team considers performing a cholecystectomy if feasible.
The surgical team adjusts the view to show the right liver, preparing to address lymphatic structures in the area.
The surgical team is working on the twelfth group of lymph nodes, aiming to collect them en bloc. The right side of the surgical field is shown for better visualization.
The camera focuses on a lymph node located in the posterior region. The surgeon attempts to determine whether the structure is lymphatic or venous, adjusting the view for closer inspection.
The surgical team attempts to enter the Eto-Motive Nerve Layer for dissection, focusing on maintaining the correct anatomical layer. The camera moves closer to provide a detailed view of the area.
The surgeon positions the camera closer to visualize the left abdominal vein, ensuring not to overlap structures. The right diaphragm is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon adjusts the view to the right side, revealing a torn vein. Clipping is performed to manage the vein, with instruments visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies and prepares to dissect the left gastrointestinal artery, noting the presence of additional tissue to be collected for pathological examination. The dissection proceeds towards the crypt, with attention given to a suspect lymph node that will be sent for further analysis.

The surgeon operates through the epigastric port, carefully navigating around a lymph node and its lymphatic vessels. The surgeon identifies the twelfth lymph node on the left and adjusts the position, using a napkin to maintain clear visibility. The surgical team discusses the next steps while ensuring the napkin does not obstruct the view.

The camera zooms in to provide a closer view of the surgical field, revealing the edge of the pancreas. The surgeon requests cleaning of the harmonica with water, while adjusting the view for better visualization.

The surgeon uses a desector to work on the eleventh group of lymph nodes near the cartilaginous vein and artery. The clips are carefully handled to avoid breaking them, while the surgical field is adjusted for better visibility.

The surgeon removes a cloth covering the surgical field to reveal the border with the pancreas. The camera zooms in to provide a closer view of the anatomical structures, including the pancreas and adjacent tissues. The surgeon adjusts the position to avoid passing through crypts, ensuring a clear view of the joint area.

The surgeon instructs Dima to maneuver through the surgical field, focusing on the area around the stomach's back vessels. The view is adjusted to show the left side, with attention to cleaning and visualizing the arterial structures.

The surgeon assesses the right and left legs of the diaphragm, indicating the presence of blood. The harmonica instrument is untwisted and cleaned, while a second clamp is used to manage the situation. The team moves to the front section, carefully spreading the tissue with the harmonica.

The surgical team pauses the operation due to technical issues with the broadcast. After resolving the issue, they resume the subtotal gastrectomy, focusing on taking the gland along the small curvature to complete the lymph node dissection.

The surgeon completes the gastrectomy and examines the surgical field, showing the vessels including the vein and salivary artery. The dissection of the eleventh P group is visible, while the seventh, eighth, and sixth groups are missing. The optical port is transferred to the right, and the patient is tilted to facilitate further examination.

The surgeon creates a glowing Mezokolani window, preparing to stretch the RU loop through it. Instruments, including clamps and a harmonica, are used to manipulate and measure approximately twenty-five centimeters from the ligament of the tracer.

The surgeon prepares for a behind-the-shoulder anastomosis, ensuring the vessel is free from tension. Instruments are adjusted and repositioned, with clamps cleaned and wires shortened for optimal access. The surgical field is tilted to facilitate the procedure.

The surgeon positions the anastomosis device through the right portal in the epigastric region. The device is maneuvered to cut off the corner and prepare for anastomosis placement. The surgical team ensures the food arcade is preserved while holding the stomach in position. The device is activated, and the team waits for the completion of the anastomosis.

The surgeon uses scissors to cut tissue, adjusting the position to ensure visibility in the surgical field. The edge of the tissue is manipulated and wrapped to lie flat, with close attention paid to the clock for timing.

The surgeon aligns the incision from left to right and proceeds to the outer stage of the procedure. A limited central laparotomy is performed, and a wound protector is placed. The incision measures approximately five to six centimeters. The surgeon prepares to extract the drug and create an anastomosis through the wound protector.
Dmitry Andreevich uses his fingers to manipulate the gut, carefully threading a needle holder to perform suturing. The surgical team employs equipment from the Shostkine school of surgery.

The surgical team is on the operating table, introducing electrolyte solvents into the gut to stimulate peristalsis and reduce infusion volume. A special seam technique named after Dmitry Andreevich is being applied.
